title: Probes and control tasks
learningObjectives: Define linear probing and explain its purpose in representation evaluation.
Understand the distinction between a property being present in a representation and a property being structurally accessible.
Explain Hewitt & Liang's (2019) concept of control tasks and how they expose over-powerful probes.
Apply Minimum Description Length (MDL) probing principles to evaluate representation quality.

whyThisLesson.text:
When we want to know if a neural network has learned a specific concept (like 'parts of speech' or 'sentiment'), the standard approach is to train a simple classifier—a linear probe—on top of the network's internal representations. If the probe achieves high accuracy, we often declare victory: 'The network understands sentiment!'

But this conclusion is dangerous. What if the probe achieved high accuracy not because the network learned a clean, structured representation of sentiment, but simply because the representation space is so high-dimensional that a linear classifier can memorize the training labels? If you give a linear probe enough dimensions, it can separate almost anything. We need a way to distinguish between a probe that is genuinely extracting existing structure and a probe that is just memorizing data. This lesson introduces control tasks and MDL probing, essential tools for keeping our evaluations honest.

sections.bridgeFrom: In the previous lesson, we saw how variance metrics can trick us into thinking a representation is good when it is functionally useless. Now, we will see how classification metrics can trick us into thinking a representation is structured when it is actually just high-dimensional.

intuition.analogy: Imagine a detective trying to prove that a suspect (the neural network) knows the layout of a secret base (the concept).
intuition.analogyLimit: Unlike human memory, which is lossy, a high-dimensional vector space can perfectly memorize random information if given enough capacity.
intuition.text:
The detective asks the suspect a series of questions. The suspect answers them all correctly. The detective concludes: 'Aha! You know the layout.' But wait—what if the detective was secretly whispering the answers to the suspect? Or what if the suspect just has an eidetic memory and memorized the questions and answers beforehand without understanding the layout?

In our neural network evaluations, the linear probe is the detective. If the probe is too powerful, or if the representation space is too large, the probe essentially 'whispers the answers' by finding complex, meaningless correlations that happen to separate the training data. To test if the suspect actually knows the layout, the detective should ask questions about a FAKE layout. If the suspect still answers them all correctly, we know they are just memorizing, not understanding. This is exactly what a control task does: we ask the probe to predict random, fake labels. If it succeeds, the probe is too powerful.

toyExample.description: Evaluate two different representations (A and B) using a standard probe and a control task to determine which representation actually has better structure.

steps.action: Train a linear probe on Representation A to predict a real property (e.g., noun vs. verb). Accuracy: 92%.
steps.insight: High raw accuracy is encouraging but inconclusive.

steps.action: Train a linear probe on Representation B to predict the same real property. Accuracy: 78%.
steps.result: Representation B seems worse than Representation A.
steps.insight: A naive evaluation would select Representation A.

steps.action: Run a control task: train the probe on Representation A to predict random, fake labels. Accuracy: 85%.
steps.insight: Representation A has high capacity but little actual structure related to the property. The probe is just memorizing.

steps.action: Run a control task on Representation B using the same random labels. Accuracy: 12%.
steps.insight: Representation B is MUCH better. Its structure genuinely supports the real property, while preventing the memorization of random noise.

visual.question: How does probe selectivity change as the complexity (capacity) of the probe increases?
visual.description: A graph showing probe accuracy on real tasks vs control tasks as probe complexity increases. As complexity grows, real task accuracy goes up, but control task accuracy goes up even faster, causing selectivity (the gap between them) to plummet. The best probes have high selectivity, not just high raw accuracy.

technical.text: Linear probing involves training a classifier $y = Wz + b$ on frozen representations $z$ to predict a property $y$. Hewitt & Liang (2019) introduced control tasks, where the true labels $y$ are replaced with random labels drawn from the same empirical distribution. Selectivity is defined as the difference between the accuracy on the real task and the accuracy on the control task. Voita & Titov (2020) proposed Minimum Description Length (MDL) probing, which evaluates representations based on how much they compress the labels. A representation that requires fewer bits to transmit the labels is considered better, as it demonstrates that the structure of the representation genuinely aligns with the task, rather than just providing enough dimensions for memorization.
definitions: Linear Probe: A simple linear classifier trained on frozen internal representations to predict a specific property.
Control Task: A diagnostic task where the real labels are replaced with random labels to test the memorization capacity of the representation and probe.
Selectivity: The difference in performance between a real task and a control task.
MDL Probing: Evaluating representation quality by measuring the compression of labels, rather than raw classification accuracy.

mathematics.label: Probe Selectivity

symbols.Accuracy(Task_{real}): Probe accuracy on the actual property
symbols.Accuracy(Task_{control}): Probe accuracy when trained on randomized labels

mathematics.plainExplanation: Selectivity is the gap between how well the probe learns the actual concept and how well it can memorize random noise.
mathematics.whyNeeded: It prevents us from being fooled by high-dimensional representations that can perfectly separate any random assignment of labels.
mathematics.ifRemoved: We would consistently overrate high-dimensional representations and overly complex probes, mistaking memorization capacity for genuine learned structure.
mathematics.encourages: Selecting representations where the specific geometry aligns with the concept, making the concept easy to learn while keeping random noise hard to learn.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: It does not guarantee that the model actually *uses* the information for its internal computations (causal faithfulness).

evidence.claim: High probe accuracy can be achieved even when the representation has no specific structure for the task.
evidence.label: [Paper Finding]
evidence.paraphrase: Standard linear probes can achieve high accuracy on complex linguistic tasks purely by memorizing data in high-dimensional spaces, making raw accuracy an unreliable indicator of learned linguistic structure unless compared against a control task.

evidence.claim: Compression provides a more principled measure of representation quality than accuracy.
evidence.label: [Paper Finding]
evidence.paraphrase: By measuring the Minimum Description Length (the number of bits required to transmit the labels given the representations), we can accurately rank representations by how much structural information they contain about the task, penalizing representations that merely offer high memorization capacity.

failureModes.name: The Powerful Probe Fallacy
failureModes.mechanism: Using a probe that is too complex (e.g., an MLP or a linear probe on a massive hidden state) to evaluate a representation.
failureModes.symptom: The probe achieves 99% accuracy on the concept, leading to the false conclusion that the model has deeply 'understood' the concept, when in fact the probe did all the learning.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Run a control task with random labels. If accuracy remains high, the probe is too powerful.
failureModes.mitigation: Always calculate Selectivity. Use MDL probing to evaluate the compression ratio. Constrain the probe's complexity.

setconcaConnection.relevance: SetConCA aims to improve the alignment of features with human-interpretable concepts.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: How we prove that SetConCA features are actually better than baseline SAE features.
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: Train linear probes on both SetConCA features and standard SAE features for a specific concept. If SetConCA has higher probe accuracy, run a control task. If SetConCA also has higher Selectivity and a better MDL compression ratio, we have robust evidence that SetConCA provides genuinely superior conceptual structure, not just higher capacity.

exercises.prompt: You increase the dimensionality of your model's hidden state from 1000 to 4000. What do you expect will happen to probe accuracy on the real task? What will happen to control task accuracy? What will happen to selectivity?
exercises.reveal: Real task accuracy will likely increase or stay high. Control task accuracy will drastically increase because the higher dimensionality provides more capacity for memorization. Therefore, Selectivity will likely DECREASE, exposing that the raw accuracy gain is an illusion.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Why might a representation with 78% accuracy and 66% selectivity be preferred over a representation with 92% accuracy and 7% selectivity?
rubric: Defines selectivity as the gap between real task accuracy and control task accuracy.
Explains that 7% selectivity means the representation is mostly just memorizing labels due to high capacity.
Explains that 66% selectivity means the representation geometry is specifically structured to support the real task.
Concludes that the 78% accuracy representation is more genuinely aligned with the concept, whereas the 92% accuracy is a mirage of capacity.
masteryQuestions.badVersion: Because 66% is a better selectivity number.
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: The representation with 92% accuracy and 7% selectivity has an 85% accuracy on random control tasks. This means the representation space is so high-dimensional or the probe is so powerful that it can separate almost any random assignment of labels. It hasn't learned the specific structure of the task; it just has massive memorization capacity. The representation with 78% accuracy and 66% selectivity only gets 12% on control tasks. This proves that its geometry specifically encodes the structure of the real task, making the true labels much easier to learn than random noise. The 78% representation genuinely 'understands' the concept better.

bridgeToNext.text: Probes help us evaluate if a specific concept is present in a representation. But what if we want to compare two entire representation spaces to see if they are structurally similar, regardless of specific concepts? In the next lesson, we'll dive into CKA and SVCCA.
